context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Trade, Outsourcing, and Labor Standards

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is openness to trade directly correlated to economic growth? What does the historical record say so far? Is the increasing shift to bilateral and regional trade arrangements compatible with the possibility of international trade, and with increasing commitment to labor rights? What do we make of the economic development strategies of emerging powers like China and India in terms of trade openness and commitment to labor standards? Who monitors labor standards?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on this trade/outsourcing/labor standards reflection tbh. can u just explain the whole prompt kinda broadly first?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you work through it! Before I do anything, tell me: what's your first read of the prompt? What do you think the core question or tension is asking you to explore?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a broad explanation of the whole prompt, which would essentially pre-digest the material for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to get them to engage with the prompt themselves. I'll ask them to identify what parts of the prompt they find confusing or challenging, so they take ownership of the direction. I want to keep it bite-sized and not over-scaffold.

exchanges.student: idk, it feels like it’s asking like 4 diff things at once lol. that didn’t narrow it much for me—can u just give me the big picture first?
exchanges.tutor: You're actually onto something — there ARE multiple questions. But instead of me connecting them, try this: read the questions one by one and ask yourself, what is the common subject running through all of them? What word or idea keeps coming back?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is pushing back and asking me to give the big picture, which would mean pre-digesting the material for them. I should resist this and instead help them see that the prompt's multiple questions are actually connected. I'll acknowledge their observation that there are multiple questions, and ask them to try to identify what connects them. This is a guided discovery move that keeps the student doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: maybe trade? or like development? ngl i’m still kinda lost rn. different angle i guess—what’s the main argument people usually make here?
exchanges.tutor: Good — trade and development are exactly the connective tissue. Now, the prompt isn't asking you to report what 'people usually say.' It's asking for YOUR reflection. So let's start small: do YOU think more trade automatically means more economic growth? What's your gut instinct, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified 'trade' and 'development' as connecting threads but is still trying to get me to give them the standard argument. I should affirm their identification of the themes without giving the answer, and redirect them to the prompt itself rather than to 'what people usually say.' I want to push them to engage with their own knowledge and the readings.

exchanges.student: my gut says “kinda but not always,” which feels super basic lol. can u just tell me what the historical record says so i have smth to react to?
exchanges.tutor: "Kinda but not always" is actually a more sophisticated position than you think — don't sell it short. Now back it up: can you think of ANY country, from your readings or general knowledge, where trade openness led to growth? Or one where it didn't go as expected?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again trying to get me to provide content for them to react to, which would essentially be doing part of the assignment for them. I should resist this and validate their 'kinda but not always' intuition as actually quite substantive, then push them to support it with examples from their readings or general knowledge. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: china maybe grew w trade, but i’m fuzzy on the details fr. quick tracking note: examples worked a little, but i still need the broad historical summary first.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but providing the summary is exactly what I'm here NOT to do — that's your job in the reflection! China is a great instinct though. What do you remember about HOW China grew? Was it simply 'open trade' or was there something more specific about how they managed it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persisting in asking me to provide the historical summary, and is even trying to instruct me on pedagogy. I need to firmly but kindly hold my boundary here, remind them of my role, and keep pushing them to work from what they know. China is a great starting point — I should push them to develop that example further rather than giving them a summary. I'll also gently push back on their attempt to redirect me.

exchanges.student: i honestly don’t remember if it was just openness or state control or both tbh. can u at least give me a super short version of the historical pattern so i don’t go in circles?
exchanges.tutor: I won't give you the summary — that's a firm boundary for me as your tutor. But here's what I'd suggest: go back to your required readings for this topic. What readings were assigned for the trade/labor standards week? Start there and come back with what you found. That's the move to make right now.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for the historical summary again — this is now the third or fourth time they've asked for me to provide content. I need to firmly hold my boundary while being empathetic. Rather than going in circles, I should redirect them to their course readings, which is the appropriate source for this content. I should also point out that going back to readings is part of the assignment process.

exchanges.student: ugh i don’t have the readings on me rn, so i’m kinda blocked. can u help me infer a possible thesis from just the prompt?
exchanges.tutor: You're not as blocked as you think — the prompt itself gives you a lot to work with. Look at this part: it asks about the 'historical record' AND about China and India specifically. What does the fact that the prompt singles out China and India suggest to you about what kind of answer it's looking for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now asking me to help them construct a thesis without doing the reading, which I should not do. However, I can redirect them productively — the prompt itself contains enough information to reason through, and I can push them to extract meaning from the prompt's own language without me giving them content. I should also gently push back on the idea that not having readings means they're blocked — they have their own knowledge and the prompt itself.

exchanges.student: maybe that trade openness isn’t one-size-fits-all, since china/india did diff stuff? if that’s right, i still need help turning that into an actual thesis sentence.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a strong central insight — hold onto it. Now, writing the thesis sentence is your job, not mine. Take that idea and try drafting something. It doesn't have to be perfect on the first try — just give it a shot and share it with me.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a genuinely good inferential leap — 'trade openness isn't one-size-fits-all' is a strong central idea. However, they're now asking me to help them write a thesis sentence, which I should not do — the writing is entirely theirs. I should affirm the idea strongly and push them to try writing it themselves. This is also a good moment to remind them that the writing must come from them.

exchanges.student: ok maybe: “trade openness can support growth, but history shows it depends on state strategy and can conflict w labor rights”? kinda rough tho.
exchanges.tutor: That's a genuinely good start — it captures the growth/strategy tension well. But look back at the full prompt: your thesis covers maybe half of it. What about bilateral/regional trade deals and who monitors labor standards? Do those fit into your thesis yet?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced a solid thesis draft on their own. I should affirm its merits genuinely without grading it, and push them to think about whether it actually covers all the parts of the prompt — notably the bilateral/regional trade arrangements question and the labor standards monitoring question, which aren't yet in the thesis. This moves the conversation forward productively.

exchanges.student: not really lol, that’s why i keep wanting the broader version. can u show me like a sample 1–2 sentence thesis that includes those too?
exchanges.tutor: I won't write a sample — that's your work to do. But you already identified what's missing: bilateral deals and labor monitoring. You have a thesis draft. Can you try adding just one of those two missing pieces to what you already wrote?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write a sample thesis for them, which I absolutely cannot do — the rules are clear that I should not write, suggest language, or provide partial solutions. I should firmly decline, acknowledge their frustration, but push them to try expanding their existing thesis themselves. They already have a good foundation — they just need to add the missing elements they themselves identified.
